raw_results.question: What are the two pre-training objectives used in BERT?
raw_results.ground_truth: BERT uses masked language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP) as its two pre-training objectives.
raw_results.answer: BERT uses two pre-training objectives: masked language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP). [Source: RoBERTa.pdf | Section: 7 Conclusion | p.10] MLM involves selecting a random sample of tokens in the input sequence and replacing them with the special token [MASK], then predicting the masked tokens using a cross-entropy loss. NSP aims to predict whether a given pair of sentences forms a coherent paragraph, where the first sentence is taken from the input sequence and the second is randomly selected from the same document. [Source: RoBERTa.pdf | Section: 2.3 Training Objectives | p.2]

According to the comparison above, AR language modeling and BERT possess their unique advan-

raw_results.question: How does the Transformer's self-attention mechanism compute attention scores?
raw_results.ground_truth: Self-attention computes attention scores using dot products of query and key vectors, scaled by the square root of the key dimension, then applies softmax to get weights over value vectors.
raw_results.answer:
The Transformer's self-attention mechanism computes attention scores as follows:
Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QKT / √dk)V [Source: Attention_Is_All_You_Need.pdf | Section: 2 Background | p.2]. This computation involves multiplying query vectors Q with key vectors K, normalizing the result by dividing by the square root of the dimensionality of the keys (dk), and applying the softmax function to obtain the attention weights. These weights are then used to compute the final output sequence by taking a weighted average of the input sequence elements.

raw_results.question: What is the key difference between LoRA and full fine-tuning?
raw_results.ground_truth: LoRA freezes the pretrained model weights and injects trainable low-rank decomposition matrices into each layer, significantly reducing the number of trainable parameters compared to full fine-tuning.
raw_results.answer: The key difference between LoRA and full fine-tuning lies in the memory cost and computational efficiency. Empirically, as observed in Table 2 of the provided document, LoRA achieves a significant reduction in memory cost compared to full fine-tuning. Specifically, LoRA reduces memory usage by up to 1.8× while still maintaining comparable performance to full fine-tuning. This is evident from Figure 1, which shows that LoRA closes the accuracy gap between conventional LoRA and full fine-tuning, yet maintains a lower memory footprint. At the time of publication, LoRA was noted to have a mean success rate of 62.1% ± 7.9% with only 914.2 million trainable parameters, consuming 64.0 GB of GPU memory, whereas full fine-tuning required 163.3 GB of GPU memory [Source: LongLoRA.pdf | Section: 3.3 IMPROVED LORA FOR LONG CONTEXT | p.5].

raw_results.question: How does FlashAttention reduce memory usage compared to standard attention?
raw_results.ground_truth: FlashAttention uses tiling and recomputation to avoid materializing the full attention matrix in GPU HBM, reducing memory from O(N^2) to O(N) while maintaining exact attention computation.
raw_results.answer: FlashAttention reduces memory usage by employing tiling techniques during the forward pass. Specifically, it loads blocks of inputs from High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) to Static Random Access Memory (SRAM), computes attention within those blocks, and updates the output without writing large intermediate matrices S and P back to HBM. This approach significantly reduces the amount of memory reads and writes, leading to a memory footprint that grows linearly with sequence length, as opposed to quadratically in standard attention mechanisms. FlashAttention achieves up to 20× more memory efficiency than exact attention baselines and is more memory-efficient than approximate attention baselines [Source: FlashAttention_2.pdf | Section: 2 Background | p.2].

raw_results.question: What is the main contribution of the RAG paper by Lewis et al.?
raw_results.ground_truth: RAG combines parametric memory (a pre-trained seq2seq model) with non-parametric memory (a dense vector index of Wikipedia) for knowledge-intensive NLP tasks.
raw_results.answer: The main contribution of the RAG paper by Lewis et al. (2020) is to enhance the input questions of generative language models with retrieved documents, thereby improving their performance in knowledge-intensive tasks. Specifically, it leverages a specific corpus, such as Wikipedia, to provide an extra knowledge source, which enhances the overall effectiveness of the language models. This approach aims to reduce hallucinations and improve the factual nature of the generated text [Source: RAG_Lewis_et_al.pdf | Section: Acknowledgments | p.10 | score: 0.711].

raw_results.question: How does Self-RAG differ from standard RAG in terms of retrieval strategy?
raw_results.ground_truth: Self-RAG trains the model to adaptively retrieve passages on-demand by generating special reflection tokens, rather than always retrieving at every generation step.
raw_results.answer: Self-RAG differs from standard RAG in that it evaluates the necessity of retrieval based on different scenarios, whereas standard RAG employs a fixed retrieval process. Self-RAG demonstrates a more flexible architecture that can easily integrate with other technologies such as fine-tuning or reinforcement learning, unlike standard RAG which may require more rigid integration methods. [Source: RAG_Survey_2024.pdf | Section: D. RAG vs Fine-tuning | p.5]

raw_results.question: How does Diffusion Policy represent robot action distributions?
raw_results.ground_truth: Diffusion Policy represents robot actions as a conditional denoising diffusion process, modeling the action distribution as the output of a denoising diffusion probabilistic model conditioned on observations.
raw_results.answer: Diffusion Policy represents robot action distributions by learning the gradient of the action-score function and iteratively optimizing with respect to this gradient field during inference via a series of stochastic Langevin dynamics steps [Source: Diffusion_Policy.pdf | Section: Learning via Action Diffusion | p.1 | score: 0.881]. This approach allows robot policies to inherit key properties from diffusion models, significantly improving performance and enabling the expression of multimodal action distributions [Source: Diffusion_Policy.pdf | Section: Learning via Action Diffusion | p.1 | score: 0.881].

raw_results.question: How does CLIP align visual and textual representations?
raw_results.ground_truth: CLIP trains an image encoder and text encoder jointly using a contrastive loss on a large dataset of image-text pairs from the internet, maximizing similarity for matching pairs and minimizing it for non-matching pairs.
raw_results.answer: CLIP aligns visual and textual representations through contrastive learning. Specifically, CLIP learns a joint representation space for text and images by leveraging contrastive objectives. This allows it to capture both semantics and style of images, enabling robust image representations [Source: CLIP_Adapter.pdf | Section: 2 Related Work | p.3]. The model has achieved impressive results across various vision tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in multimodal reasoning [Source: CLIP_Adapter.pdf | Section: 2 Related Work | p.3].

raw_results.question: What is the chain-of-thought prompting technique?
raw_results.ground_truth: Chain-of-thought prompting elicits multi-step reasoning in large language models by providing few-shot examples that include intermediate reasoning steps before the final answer.
raw_results.answer: Chain-of-thought prompting is a technique that guides large language models to produce a sequence of intermediate reasoning steps before providing the final answer. This method is designed to enhance the complex reasoning capabilities of language models. It involves providing a few examples, known as chain-of-thought demonstrations, as exemplars in the prompting process. These examples serve as a guide for the model to follow, enabling it to solve problems that require multiple steps of reasoning. The technique is particularly effective in improving performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks. At the time of the publication, chain-of-thought prompting showed significant improvements over standard prompting methods, especially when applied to large language models like PaLM 540B [Source: Chain-of-Thought_Prompting.pdf | Section: in Large Language Models | p.1 | score: 0.842].

ing, natural language explanations, program synthesis/execution, numeric and logical reasoning, and
intermediate language steps.
C.1
Prompting
The recent success of large-scale language models has led to growing interest in improving their
capability to perform tasks via prompting (Brown et al. (2020), and see Liu et al. (2021) for a
survey). This paper falls in the category of general prompting approaches, whereby input prompts are
optimized to allow a single large language model to better perform a variety of tasks (Li and Liang,
2021; Lester et al., 2021; Reif et al., 2022, inter alia).
One recent line of work aims to improve the ability of language models to perform a task by providing
instructions that describe the task (Raffel et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2022a; Ouyang et al., 2022; Sanh

et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2022b). In this work, we refer to this technique as chain-of-thought prompting. In
the few-shot setting, these intermediate reasoning steps are manually written for the few-shot exemplars,
and the model will then generate its own chain-of-thoughts for the test examples. Only the ﬁnal answer is
used for evaluation, although these generated chain-of-thoughts can be useful for error analysis and model
interpretability. An example of chain-of-thought prompting for the GSM8K benchmark (grade-school math

Multi-step reasoning. Reasoning tasks, especially those involving multiple steps, have been challenging for
language models and NLP models more broadly (Rae et al., 2021; Bommasani et al., 2021; Nye et al., 2021). A
recent prompting strategy called chain-of-thought prompting enables language models to solve such problems
by guiding them to produce a sequence of intermediate steps before giving the ﬁnal answer (Cobbe et al., 2021;
Wei et al., 2022b; Suzgun et al., 2022). As shown in Figure 3A, chain of thought prompting only surpasses
standard prompting without intermediate steps when scaled to 1023 training FLOPs (∼100B parameters).
A similar emergence in performance gain was also observed when augmenting few-shot prompting with
explanations that came after the ﬁnal answer (Lampinen et al., 2022).

Abstract
We explore how generating a chain of thought—a series of intermediate reasoning
steps—signiﬁcantly improves the ability of large language models to perform
complex reasoning. In particular, we show how such reasoning abilities emerge
naturally in sufﬁciently large language models via a simple method called chain-of-
thought prompting, where a few chain of thought demonstrations are provided as
exemplars in prompting.
Experiments on three large language models show that chain-of-thought prompting
improves performance on a range of arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic
reasoning tasks. The empirical gains can be striking. For instance, prompting a
PaLM 540B with just eight chain-of-thought exemplars achieves state-of-the-art

chain-of-thought prompting by constructing for each prompting method a simple prompt context that
contains a single example that is solvable with just 2 reasoning steps. We then evaluate accuracy
on examples that may involve larger numbers of reasoning steps. The same example is used for
both prompting methods. For the least-to-most prompting prompt, we adopt a simpliﬁed approach
in which the problem decomposition and solution stages are merged into a single pass, with just one
follow-up request being made to the language model to solicit the ﬁnal answer.

raw_results.question: How does DeepSeek-R1 train reasoning capabilities without supervised chain-of-thought data?
raw_results.ground_truth: DeepSeek-R1 uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement learning with accuracy and format rewards to develop reasoning capabilities from scratch, then applies rejection sampling and supervised fine-tuning for distillation.
raw_results.answer: DeepSeek-R1 trains reasoning capabilities through a multi-stage learning framework that integrates reinforcement learning (RL). Specifically, the authors apply RL on the DeepSeek-V3 base model to train DeepSeek-R1-Zero. During training, DeepSeek-R1-Zero is required to produce a reasoning process followed by the final answer, with constraints designed to observe its natural progression during the RL process. This approach allows the model to develop advanced reasoning patterns such as self-reflection, verification, and dynamic strategy adaptation without the need for supervised chain-of-thought data [Source: DeepSeek_R1.pdf | Section: 2.3. Incentivize Reasoning Capability in LLMs | p.4].

2.3. Incentivize Reasoning Capability in LLMs
Specifically, we apply the RL technique on the DeepSeek-V3 base to train DeepSeek-R1-Zero.
During training, we design a straightforward template, to require DeepSeek-R1-Zero to first
produce a reasoning process, followed by the final answer. We intentionally limit our constraints
to this structural format, avoiding any content-specific biases to ensure that we can accurately
observe the model’s natural progression during the RL process.

DeepSeek-R1-Zero struggles with challenges like poor readability, and language mixing, as
DeepSeek-V3-Base is trained on multiple languages, especially English and Chinese. To address
these issues, we develop DeepSeek-R1, whose pipeline is illustrated in Figure 2.
In the initial stage, we collect thousands of cold-start data that exhibits a conversational,
human-aligned thinking process. RL training is then applied to improve the model perfor-
mance with the conversational thinking process and language consistency. Subsequently, we
apply rejection sampling and SFT once more. This stage incorporates both reasoning and non-
reasoning datasets into the SFT process, enabling the model to not only excel in reasoning tasks
but also demonstrate advanced writing capabilities. To further align the model with human
preferences, we implement a secondary RL stage designed to enhance the model’s helpfulness
and harmlessness while simultaneously refining its reasoning capabilities.

raw_results.question: What does Llama Guard classify?
raw_results.ground_truth: Llama Guard is an LLM-based input-output safeguard model that classifies safety risks in both human prompts and AI responses according to a predefined taxonomy of harm categories.
raw_results.answer: Llama Guard classifies text inputs into "safe" or "unsafe" categories based on the specified output format. It outputs "safe" or "unsafe" as single tokens in the SentencePiece tokenizer, and if the assessment is "unsafe," it lists the violated taxonomy categories on a new line [Source: Llama_Guard.pdf | Section: 3.2 Zero-shot and Few-shot Prompting | p.4].

raw_results.question: What is the core representation used in 3D Gaussian Splatting?
raw_results.ground_truth: 3D Gaussian Splatting represents scenes as a collection of 3D Gaussian primitives with learnable position, covariance, opacity, and spherical harmonic color coefficients, enabling real-time differentiable rasterization.
raw_results.answer: The core representation used in 3D Gaussian Splatting is 3D Gaussian splats. raw_results.question: What architectural choices does MobileLLM make to optimize language models for mobile devices?
raw_results.ground_truth: MobileLLM prioritizes model depth over width, uses embedding sharing and grouped query attention, and applies immediate block-wise weight sharing to improve parameter efficiency for sub-billion parameter language models.
raw_results.answer: MobileLLM makes several architectural choices to optimize language models for mobile devices. These include adopting deep and thin architectures, embedding sharing mechanisms, and grouped-query attention mechanisms. The authors note that these designs lead to a remarkable 2.7% and 4.3% accuracy boosts over preceding 125M and 350M state-of-the-art models, respectively, as of the time of publication [Source: MobileLLM.pdf | Section: 1. Introduction | p.1 | score: 0.856]. Additionally, MobileLLM-LS demonstrates further enhancements of 0.7% and 0.8% over MobileLLM 125M/350M, showcasing significant improvements on chat benchmarks and API calling tasks compared to previous sub-billion models [Source: MobileLLM.pdf | Section: 3.3.1 | p.7 | score: 0.824].

Conclusion
In this paper, we introduce TinyLlama, an open-source, small-scale language model. With its compact
architecture and promising performance, TinyLlama can enable end-user applications on mobile
devices, and serve as a lightweight platform for testing a wide range of innovative ideas related
to language models. We also additionally verify the effectiveness of multi-stage training and data
schedule through our v1.1 series. To promote transparency in the open-source language model
pre-training community, we have released all relevant information, including our pre-training code,
intermediate model checkpoints, and the details of our data processing steps. We hope that TinyLlama
will make a valuable contribution to the community as we aim to democratize language model
research and provide useful insights for future research in this field.
